summary: Luu challenges the efficient market hypothesis by examining decision-making in baseball and board games, where outcomes are easily measurable and data is abundant. He demonstrates that even with clear statistical evidence showing decisions cost teams multiple wins per season (worth millions of dollars), managers persisted with demonstrably inferior strategies like excessive sac bunts, stolen base attempts, and intentional walks for decades. Despite financial incentives, accessible data, and rigorous analysis available since the 1970s, cultural resistance and tolerance for high-variance outcomes delayed adoption of evidence-based decision-making until the 2000s-2010s, suggesting similar or worse decision quality in less measurable business and engineering domains.
key_points: Statistical analysis shows conventional baseball decisions (sac bunts, steals, intentional walks, batting order) cost teams 3-5 wins per season, worth tens of millions in player value
Despite clear evidence since the 1970s and massive financial incentives, teams took decades to adopt data-driven strategies, with some decisions only improving after 2012
Board game players confidently advocate losing strategies despite having publicly visible Elo ratings proving their poor performance
High variance in outcomes makes it easy to justify bad decisions and penalizes managers who make statistically correct but unconventional choices
If decision-making is this poor in easily measurable domains with clear incentives, it's likely worse in business and engineering where outcomes are far more ambiguous
money_quotes: A statement I commonly hear in tech-utopian circles is that some seeming inefficiency can't actually be inefficient because the market is efficient and inefficiencies will quickly be eliminated.

Stats nerds were talking about how you could do this at least since 1971 and no team really took it seriously until the 90s and the ideas didn't really become mainstream until the mid 2000s, after a bestseller had been published.

The thing that's odd about this is, how come the low-ranked players so confidently argue that their position is correct? Not only do they get per-game information indicating that they're wrong (because they often lose), they have a rating that aggregates all of their gameplay and tells them, roughly, how good they are.

You can't even tell if this is happening by asking people if they're making rigorous, data-driven, decisions. If you'd ask people in baseball they were using data in their decisions, they would've said yes throughout the 70s and 80s.

It's too simplistic to say that we expect a firm to make good decisions because they're exposed to markets and there's economic value in making good decisions and people within the firm will probably be rewarded greatly if they make good decisions.
key_insight: Strong financial incentives and readily available data are insufficient to drive good decision-making when cultural inertia, high variance outcomes, and resistance to unconventional ideas allow decision-makers to dismiss clear statistical evidence for decades.
